C[topazg: It's often tempting to play around the board more quickly. Learning which defences are important is good - particularly when it comes from a game that gets very tricky as a result like this one :)
Katokoda: I thought you were gonna play H8 to make sure both centers stay in touch
Katokoda: But, you prefer to weaken my center group, that's fair enough
C[topazg: Obviously I do want to keep them in touch, but I want to make eyespace whilst I do it :)
Katokoda: Are some of them bad? Did I miss something?
topazg: All of them are good areas, and certainly all of the important ones
topazg: I'd prefer P17 to P18, as with P18 I push and then block, and it's sort of sente for me. When you've identified a few big points, you want sente as often as possible
topazg: Likewise F15 is a good point - but can you play up there, achieving what you want from F15, but in sente? Always look to keep control of the flow of the game.
C[Katokoda: E16 is similar but more treathening
Katokoda: I do not know if it is really sente, though
topazg: Yeah, E16 is better, and you have another move (not going to give too much away at this point!)
Katokoda: you mean that my E16 seems sente, but when you play F17 I actually have to defend something, right?
C[topazg: Yes, exactly. Sente is ""who can tenuki"", just because someone has to respond to a move you make, it's only sente if you don't then also have to respond to their move
Katokoda: Yeah I am slightly surprised too TT
C[topazg: Yeah, probably I'd look at doing 4 in your line @ 5
topazg: But this also needs careful reading now :)
C[topazg: Have a great game again!

topazg: My goal here isn't to kill your stones (although if I can that's valuable), but to make sure you aren't making territory in this area - so getting my stones to safety is good enough for me
Katokoda: Thing is, killing my stones makes you safe enough

C[topazg: Yeah, those all look reasonable. Try to evaluate them based on ""whether they're definitely sente for you"" or whether ""I have a move in sente there that removes them""
topazg: Picking those large / endgame moves based on sente and reverse sente is a good habit :)
C[Katokoda: Well I was sure my move was sente bahaha
C[topazg: It's not small - but it's less sente now that you've saved the H13 etc group, and my K8 group is at least connected / alive
topazg: I would always respond if I thought I could play O11 and kill those stones, but I can't make that work, so I decided to do something I thought was bigger instead :)
Katokoda: ululu and now there is some big shenanigans happening
topazg: This is well worth a close look, there are often these sort of semi-connecting sequences that need to be evaluated when there is a possibility of connection on both sides
C[Katokoda: I decided I want to kill O16 in order to make sure my M16 little group is safe. I believe I can block you from connecting to both sides, but you will easily connect to one.
C[topazg: Yeah, that's how these moves work, miai of connection. Probably the best bet normally is to push one side (like R18 or L18 instead of O17
topazg: But in this case you need to worry about N16 cuts, in lines like this:
Katokoda: Yes. this is why I took my move back. Figured out that your stones were difficult to kill, I always had 1-2 liberties left

topazg: As a rule of thumb, you play O1 instead of N1 if N1 ends in gote. I play this because I think it's sente :)

C[topazg: Correct, the game theory behind it is ""I just need to play it before you playing P2 in reverse sente is the biggest move on the board""

topazg: As long as you remain happy to sacrifice individual stones that you just used to force with, they apply a lot of pressure. It's just important to remember you don't necessarily have to keep them alive unless it really helps you to save them :)
C[topazg: Have a great game again !
C[idleparty: Well played in the last one - a convincing win

C[topazg: Thank you, I was slightly lucky. The fight on the left hand side was exactly what White wants, a multi-group fight that keeps escalating, and then Black feels like something important has to be sacrificed
topazg: It is hard to create a fight where a) a lot is on the line, and b) I'm not hopelessly worse at the beginning of it !
C[idleparty: Yes, I was deliberately trying to be more attacking and applying 'cut first, think later'. Clearly more selectivity was required.
C[topazg: It was difficult though, because I think the way you started the fight made total sense
topazg: Because of the handicap, you probably could have chosen not to and still been clearly ahead at that point
topazg: But cutting is probably the _right_ move, it's just very complicated and leads to a long running fight where the price of going wrong is very high

topazg: That was sort of what I was trying to say really, the game ended up in a situation where you played the right way, but the chance of it going badly in an expensive way was a lot better than the average sequence in our handicap games
topazg: (better for me I mean, although the corollary was that it going really badly for me was also higher)

C[redacted: With the shape of C4 and R4 ""kenka komoku"" the first move on the bottom side is important. Thus the play C4 is questionable.
C[redacted: Enclosing the upper left corner (C15 perhaps) is the biggest play. In the lower left, simply connect with E4. Also big is wariuchi at R10.
C[redacted: Black at C12 instead is also thinkable.
C[redacted: White should defend and attack with G5 or similar. The kosumitsuke of L3 leaves significant weaknesses.
C[redacted: White's sacrifice idea is good, Black is difficult now.
C[redacted: White should take sente with  R14 etc.
C[redacted: White defends this group very well.
C[redacted: Black wants to separate the white stones.
C[redacted: Attack is difficult now, so Black takes a large point.
C[redacted: White should consider playing double sente at S15 or S9.

C[topazg: This move really doesn't seem to gain black anything. If you are to approach my stone, play c4 and approach from the side where you can gain territory (on the left edge)
C[topazg: When I play at G5, I am going to be trying to live on the edge. Make that harder for me with G6, G4, H6 or H4. This just forces me to charge up and down the edge of the board.

C[topazg: Ugly ugly shape. Now we see how poor the E4 move was -- it has completely failed to contribute anything to the game. Better here would be h3, making it hard to make eyespace with the white stones.

C[topazg: Total waste of a move here, earned you 0 points. Much better would be bH6 (wH5) bJ7 (wJ4) and then go elsewhere.

You can also simply jump to c4, and attack my remaining group. With the strength of your wall and the c7 stone you should be able to get something from the fight.

C[topazg: Again, playing in the middle like this is really small, unless I have no choice but to defend against it. c4 is better, as is b5 -- Look towards the 2nd and 3rd lines much more at this point in the game.
C[topazg: Just connect at F3. If you had a stone at D4 this would threaten two cuts and be an excellent move - as it is I just get another free sente move.

C[minompi: Have a nice game too
C[minompi: It's a pleasure to play against the founder of ogs :)
C[topazg: Ah, you're welcome, not sure I can claim much this many years on. I'm just glad to see it still doing so well
C[minompi: I am a software developer and I really appreciate when someone creates something that works really fine
topazg: Yeah, it's improved a lot since I made it. The current owners have done a lot of work on various parts of it and taken it a long way
topazg: When I created it I basically wanted something that could manage turn based Go, but focused on tournaments, ladders, leagues, competitions, that sort of thing
C[minompi: You have destroyed me.. Too strong for me :)

C[topazg: Ah, you were doing really well for a lot of it. I'll post a couple of comments on what were mostly overly passive defenses more than anything else, let me get a bunch of points I shouldn't have

C[topazg: Whilst this is undoubtedly big, you really don't want White to get another move in the top left. D17 will isolate F17 and give me a very small corner to live in, and a lot of influence for you to play with.
C[topazg: C3 much better, you don't want to encourage me to develop a base, especially not in the corner.

C[topazg: This is kind of a thank you move, it made D7 much more solidly connected to C4 without a particularly big gain yourself. Tenuki would be fine, something like C5 would have been locally much more devastating.
C[topazg: Very small. This gives you good endgame chances to reduce something of White's in this corner, but little else. A move like R4 would completely kill R3 and take the corner back.
C[topazg: G5 serves its purpose in giving me some central influence, whilst retaining some aji of its own.

topazg: For consistency, you should consider H5 or J6 - something locally to concrete the territory you should be able to get at the bottom - 6th line territory is amazing if you can get it, and with the stones you have in place it will be hard for me to prevent.

C[topazg: If your groups on either side of the corner are alive, this is really worth almost 0 points. My stone at F10 makes it hard to make territory here, and with the possibility of wG16 this doesn't even connect the groups. It also doesn't directly pressure White in any way.
C[topazg: Locally very worth responding to, and right placement.
C[topazg: H6 way better. You have G4 and J4 as miai separating moves, the ponnuki here was way too useful for White.
C[topazg: Slow, but hoping for a kill so the strength of the connection was useful to me here.
C[topazg: My groups are both quite strong. You can achieve the cut, but you really should look at stabilising your group first!
C[topazg: E2 works, and should give plenty of space as White cannot afford black to follow up with C2 very easily.
C[topazg: You can probably get away with P12 or R12, as they have tesujis to guarantee connecting each side. Perhaps trying to kill is more appropriate in context though, it's what I'd try to do :)
C[topazg: Now things are a bit harder, your corner group has become quite weak, and I've got a run into the center of the last remaining big area for you territory.
C[topazg: I'm not going to play here, so don't take it yourself unless you're going to cut, as forcing me to get Q11 gives me a bit of extra strength to play with.
C[topazg: Now I can approach from both sides, this is very useful for White!
C[topazg: Making sure there was no territory to be had here.

C[topazg: This has just weakened the RHS group, which was already a bit weak ! It kills the stone in a ladder, but I hadn't expected to do anything with the stone anyway. Cutting at Q6 is now very big for White as it threatens to kill the groups on both sides and fully connects White, removing the need for the R12 group to make eyes of its own.
topazg: Playing P6 or similar here yourself gives you a connection between the ponnuki and the bottom group. Moves like S13 later become much more playable after this.
C[topazg: Very good spot. This wins all around, I should never have allowed this move!
C[topazg: A crazy move. Simply P6 and White keeps the Black group dead, which wins the game. Perserverence paid off very well, congratulations :)

C[topazg: Many thanks for the game :) There is no question that your biggest issue here was responding to almost every move I made. Even in a big handicap game, try to get into the habit of ignoring your opponent and taking sente.
GN[How many rooms a tesseract has?]

C[topazg: Generally not a good idea to play shoulder hits like this. You will make White stronger after N17, which will help White attack the middle K16 stone, and Black will still want another move to defend O17 which will then be weak.
topazg: Q14 and R14 both work well at defending the corner, making a claim to more points, and do so without strengthening White's stone.
C[topazg: White now has a basis of strength from which to start attacking the Black position in the top half of the board.

C[topazg: Playing strongly to break out of White's position. You should make some central facing thickness here, but you may also make White very strong locally, and if K16 is sacrificed in the process, F17 will become much stronger. Black's O16 group is very safe, and you don't need to play this way - better to take H15 and force F17 to stay isolated.
C[topazg: H16 is more promising than this locally, but White will play F15 and try to complicate things. Giving White that extra thickness has hurt Black now.
C[topazg: These two moves are fine. You might want to consider F4, but this is good enough, putting pressure on the now heavy White stones.
C[topazg: Divide and conquer. This doesn't earn many points, and lets White break out in sente. Better to play at E8 and cut White's stones in half. Make White run, not the other way round!
C[topazg: White played on a shape weak point, but it was also overplay. wS9 connects, so make sure it doesn't - S9 would prevent the connection and make the R6 group weak

C[topazg: Saving the connection for as long as possible. Now Black has a weak-ish group facing the middle and a corner with lots of aji. White relies on having some targets to attack in a high handicap game, so splitting Black has a lot of long term use.

C[topazg: There's still a lot of aji left at Q18 - with the size and sealed in nature of that corner, playing again at Q17 is quite reasonable, and is guaranteed at least some points in profit. White can get a local gain with Q18 otherwise, and in sente.
C[topazg: Fairly typical response, and quite reasonable - watch for that eyespace though!
You could also play bP19 wP17 bQ17 wP16 (2) bR18 to guarantee life in gote if you are worried about the corner itself.
C[topazg: A massively dangerous tenuki - you have no really severe followup, and that corner is precarious!
C[topazg: This move looks like it probably kills the corner, as S19 and Q15 are miai (S19 making it a one eye vs no eye fight, Q15 forcing bR16 and creating a bulky five dead shape)
C[topazg: This may be a terrible mistake. It may be possible for Black to live with Q15 (wS19 bR16), but it's complicated. If wQ15 now there is nothing for Black after bS19 wT18.
C[topazg: The corner is now dead.

C[topazg: Playing to make a large middle, and trying to take advantage of the weakness of that middle right group. The cutting move at P14, which looked slow and optimistic at the time, has turned out to be hugely valuable.

Most of White's strategy in high handicap games comes from making Black weak in as many areas as possible, and then playing to exploit those weaknesses. Black's corresponding strategy should be to minimise his weaknesses (such as large groups that aren't completely settled), and try to keep White with a few of his own by keeping stones and groups separated -- divide and conquer!
C[topazg: Better at F5 I think - Sacrificing K4 to try to save J8 would be a worthwhile exchange, White's still clutching at straws for the game at the moment.

topazg: The corner still has aji with C3, and you want to minimise White's ability to harrass your groups. Jumping upwards to F5 threatens to kill White's D6 group as well, and you still have moves around N4 to make use of the K4 stone later (or swallow O3 if White insists on keeping K4 dead)

C[topazg: The other side may be safer. That way, if White plays J3, Black has a working ladder after G5, and if wJ4 bH5, White cannot defend G4 and H3 at the same time. This way you ask White to make things as complicated as possible.
C[topazg: Playing purely to separate and keep Black weak in as many areas as possible.

C[topazg: Now Black has an isolated middle group with no guaranteed eyes, a very weak corner, and has lost his grip on the lower edge. After H4 black's mistake was trying to keep hold of everything at once, and has ended up becoming very weak all over.

C[topazg: Allowing the capture here is risky, as White's weak group at D6 becomes unassailably strong. Make sure that bC4 here does not (cannot) work before playing it - I think it might be ok here, because of the aji against White's E3 group.

C[topazg: Allowing the capture of the two stones, but forcing Black to live small in the corner in gote, and securing the D6 stones for good by leaving them fully connected. White now eyes up the middle group for a major attack.
C[topazg: The last two moves were designed to attack the middle group in as big a way as I can
C[topazg: Now the fight really begins, as the stones have nowhere to run.
C[topazg: You have two settled groups on the right - focus on the group on the left!
C[topazg: Again, you can sacrifice those 5 stones for a loss of 10 points, perhaps 15 with aji. If you can save those middle stones - perhaps with G12, that's worth more like 50.
C[topazg: Now the middle group is simply dead.
C[topazg: At this point White starts to play passively, and hold onto the game - capturing that middle group put White about 30 points ahead.
C[topazg: Ironically, I played this as sente against the group, and then forgot for about 30 moves!
C[topazg: A good plan, but too little too late.
C[topazg: Defend with bB10 wA10 bB12 and the group cannot go any further.
C[topazg: A disastrous move! J7 and H8 are miai, White simply had to play F6 or F7 to keep the group dead. This lost 40 points in one fell swoop.
C[topazg: Excellent save - the only move that lives unconditionally :)
Eleric937: Thank you :) its about time i did a good move lol.

C[topazg: Having thought the game had been thrown away, I refound this kill as a bent-four-in-the-corner. When White played E1 Black had to respond with a move in the corner to guarantee the two eyes. Dragon Pie: Pretty sure you're stronger than me, though.

Dragon Pie: If White plays on the bottom or the top, I will enclose and treat the remaining corner as miai.  I kind of like diagonal games, so I play this way so that people will be more likely to allow them.  If he plays in the bottom left, I'll probably just take Q4 which will likely revert to orthodox.
C[topazg: Enjoy the game to you too :)

C[Dragon Pie: Since he spoiled a plan for something like orthodox, I decided to make it a diagonal fuseki.  If he takes the last empty corner, I enclose the shimari.  If he does something like invade my shimari, I have the option of taking the top left corner and having an ideal approach.
C[Dragon Pie: I'll either get an ideal extension on the right side(I doubt it.  Or an approach on the top right stone preventing a shimari.
C[Dragon Pie: My opponent sure is good at making an invasion between his last move and his star corner stone tempting.
Dragon Pie: Preventing the shimari aslo feels nice.  His stone is low, so I should have the shoulder hit later.
Dragon Pie: But, I've decided to invade.  I think that it's more urgent.  It splits his stone off and if I make a base, I'll be stronger in the area and ripe to attack his stone.  Here it goes.
C[Dragon Pie: This move probes white to choose which way he'd like to connect.  After I know, I can take sente and play a big move.  Probably the approach on his shimari is best.

C[Dragon Pie: With this move, the most natural follow up is to press my stone.  If I respond to solidify my stone he can build a position on the top side.  As it is, I've prevented him from having a shimari here, my stone has done it's job.  A move along the top is one that both of us want.  On top of that, if I get a position on the right, I may be able to pressure his group on the right.  I can't pressure it directly at the moment, but I welcome the possibility of doing so.  I will play high for improved influence and a greater chance at being able to pressure the group.  The bottom right also catches my eye as a place that I'd like to play, but alas, I only get to put one stone down at a time.

C[Dragon Pie: My 3-3 stone can be pressed, but it's also a solid position, so I like the prospects of attaching to the stones on top.  Those stones are already relatively strong and they say attach to strong groups.  This is because it'll get a bit stronger, but so will my stones which will aid me more.
C[Dragon Pie: Incidentally, I've never studied the kosumi joseki outside of seeing it in some games.

C[Dragon Pie: I can't allow him weaker stone to connect to his stronger group.  I also really need to disconnect him in sente if at all possible.  So, I play this stone and allow him to capture it if he desires.  Because sente is most important here.  I've seen this move in some game before.  I don't know if I would have thought of it on my own, but it's the type of move that I'm trying to always consider.
C[Dragon Pie: It was between this and pincering directly.  He'd likely jump up and I'd push out.  It'd make him strong and ruin my plan of harassing his weak group.

C[Dragon Pie: I thought about playing this high, but the threat to undercut his stones and leave him completely baseless is too much to pass up.  I am about to see multiple weak groups and I'm hungry, so that's good.
C[Dragon Pie: I'm sure he was aware that I was playing low to erode his base.  And of course this also threatens to cut his keima.

Dragon Pie: Ok, I changed my mind before hitting submit.  There's more aji in my position than I like and I like the idea of white worrying about my potential slide.  If he kicks to prevent it, I certainly don't mind that as it helps solidify me.  I often like to play solid and the cut at F16 starts to look appetizing.  If I have strong groups while my opponent's are weaker, I feel good.  I like my opponent to be the one straining while I relax and drink an icy margarita.

C[Dragon Pie: Well, I know that one move that isn't an option is submissively connecting my two stones and allowing black to strengthen his in the process.  That would give me a bad feeling.  But, yes, I can't let white just close me in either.
C[Dragon Pie: I can't let him entirely enclose my corner.
C[Dragon Pie: Still all about strengthening my group and leaving his relatively weak.
C[Dragon Pie: You know, I don't mind so much if he connects by ko.  This spot represents potential eyespace for both groups.  Also considered the above as well.

C[Dragon Pie: His move is a tesuji to get sente.  I considered B8 to play on the outside first leaving it gote for him to capture the stone, but then we're basically back to where we were before this began.  He'll owe another move to running away at least and I should probably back off and not over pursue.  Besides, I'd love to get M3 in.

Dragon Pie: After M3, I'm hoping I can come back and get pressure on his stones on the bottom left.  I also considered peeping at F3 and jump to H3 to just start grabbing some bottom side territory while pressuring that group a bit.  Well, I have time to think.

C[Dragon Pie: I don't mind if he connects in gote.  He'll still have two other weakish groups on the board and two moves in a row sounds nice to me.  I doubt he spends another move right now to connect up in gote.  I could never bring myself to do so.  So I love the aji of potentially cutting him off with a ko later.

Dragon Pie: As for which move I chose, I don't like my corner being closed in at G2.  It's a big move that white would like to get in.  This move strengthens him, but he'll get a worthless wall considering that my group is pretty strong on the left.  He'll still have to watch to make sure his group doesn't become weak even as it becomes a wall.  I also considered M3 to pressure that corner and somewhere on the right to pressure that group.  This play feels most urgent, but they're all like delicious candy to me.  This group has the best follow up against me though, so I go here first.
C[Dragon Pie: Perhaps he's looking forward to a ladder breaker that'll hurt my group and help his.
C[Dragon Pie: Ok, I hallucinated that sequence that gives him the ladder and the connection.  Phew.
Dragon Pie: Considered the bamboo joint for just a moment and realized that I don't like the aji if he makes me push and cut.
C[Dragon Pie: Hmmm. . . With his latest move, he's given me the chance of removing the ladder at the expense of leaving my other stone in atari.

Dragon Pie: I'd rather not have to worry about that aji and he has another peep to cover his cutting point (Which makes me think that I realy should have done a bamboo connection on the first peep, but oh well.
C[Dragon Pie: He felt he had time to defend there.  I guess
Dragon Pie: I guess I'll ask him to show me because I'm interested in learning new things.
C[Dragon Pie: This move reduces his eye space and I'm strong enough in the area and globally that the cut doesn't really bother me.

C[Dragon Pie: I don't see a reason to do any sort of silly ko stuff.  He still has to prove he can live.  His recent move mutes my influence a bit, but his corner on the bottom left still has aji.  and my center is starting to gain some possibilities.  I used to feel bad about groups living small in my area of influence, but I feel like I've gained quite a bit from these attacks in this game.  I tend to lose more often when I try to hard to kill or be too blood-thirst.  If he lives, I have several good choices of moves to play.  I think it's time to harass his top right group.  Just gotta decide how.
C[Dragon Pie: This move concerns his eyespace or lack thereof.

C[Dragon Pie: I almost feel like he's just died in gote.  C2 is the trickiest move I can imagine and I don't see a problem there.  There is a little bit of aji for a cut.  So, I'll go ahead and fix that.

C[topazg: As a general rule ""shoulder hits"" are not a good idea. Here you will give black a good wall, just what he wants facing his stones at the top, and make white overconcentrate with three stones very close together and little territoy (or even life!) to show for it.

C[topazg: Better is P7. I can't cut and it puts a lot of pressure on my P8 stone. Q7/Q8/R8 is an empty triangle, which is generally bad shape, but because of the local situation here it is becoming a very weak and heavy group.

C[topazg: This is too slow, and messy because of the shape. F7 would be better - if you can, try to run as quickly and lightly as possible.
C[topazg: J2 much better. It threatens to connect under at H1 and guarantees an eye after the following K1.

C[topazg: Try to secure your groups early, before they start to become attack on all sides. Once you have to spend 10 or 20 moves on the run the game is probably already going badly, as your opponent can gain a lot of influence and/or territory in those moves whilst harrassing your group(s).

C[topazg: D4 would probably be better here .. you really want to keep my groups split. Alternatively, C4 is fine just to secure the left side. If you play C4 and G5 you've probably won the game outright.

C[topazg: Now things are worrying for you. Not terminal, but much more complex. D7 remains a big move for you to save the top left corner without complications.

C[topazg: Again, try not to lose track of the big picture. This group is now definitely all on its own. B5 would have risked you connecting at A4. Even if I cut at C8 you play B8, and after I capture, you connect at A4. This way you lose very little for guaranteed life.

C[topazg: F9 was a dishonest move. You should be able to see that black cannot get life in the corner here. So you should play the cut at F8 even if you don't think it works. C[topazg: What considerations are there when kciking a knight's move approach to a 5-5?
Sunny Goodman: I thought a pincer would cause a 3.3 in the top left, so thought this one.

C[topazg: Yes. It probably would mean I play the 3-3. The kick makes me slightly stronger on the left, and slightly protects the 3-3. I can actually still invade the 3-3 at this point, although at some cost on the left
topazg: I would generally rather player F17 directly than kick first unless I plan on playing B14 imminently, but as long as you aware you are gaining and losing something from the kick, it's fine
C[Sunny Goodman: I see, so the invasion can still happen. But yes, I thought of it as an exchange.
C[topazg: Yes, indeed :) Otherwise your opening is absolutely fine, nothing to say really
Sunny Goodman: I recently learned these opennings, so getting familiar with when to use each one. Bit of strategy.
C[topazg: Yes, this is a pretty important basic joseki. It used to be more common before AI made the low approach so much more often, but this is obviously still excellent, and simple to understand

C[topazg: Torn slightly which side to approach on - I chose this side as I'm happy with the slide+D8 joseki, as D8 offers a more solid left hand side than the equivalent shape would on the lower side. I'm equally happy with the 3-3, and if you kick I get the ideal extension from C10 on both sides

C[topazg: Because you have no stone on the lower side to support the high move at F4, I'm going to take this point immediately. It destabilises the corner somewhat and forces you to fight or make some concessions (or both)

C[topazg: I have chosen to respond to your defence with one of my own. I also thought about D8 as it takes away some of the aji I have left at C8 and D7, but I also can now aim towards C3 _and_ E3 myself, and I felt this was slightly more important
C[Sunny Goodman: I was wondering how it would happen.

C[topazg: F3 probably protects all of the aji - If I play E3, you can block with E4 and my stone just dies. It also means that if I play C3, you can block at C4 and the F3 stone stops me having enough space in the corner to make life

topazg: The most critical point of the last few moves is how you've felt compelled to defend ""internally"". The more I can force you to respond to defend whilst taking moves on the outside that I want to play that develop my position, the more I should be able to exploit the game's opening

topazg: The other options you have would be to play C8/D7/K3 etc instead of holding onto the corner, and try to apply as much pressure to me as I'm doing to you. The chances are that I play C3 and try to take the corner, but then you should get a strong outside position. You risk being left with a small enough number of points this way that you would probably get more profit from outside thickness in the end
C[Sunny Goodman: Ok, definitely felt obliged to do something, just wanted maybe live small and be done with it quickly. Haven't learned to invade yet, but started to see where the positions are.

topazg: Q17 is fine. Q18 also fine. As K16 is where it is, you could also leave the corner for a bit - if I jump into say, M17, you can just sacrifice O17 and play R17 quite happily too. This is fine as well, it is just a different commitment
C[Sunny Goodman: I think Q18 would be better as I don't want to follow this joseki as white will use it to make influence.
Sunny Goodman: I made that mistake yesterday, still getting familiar with these.
topazg: Q18 is certainly very common. I think Q17 is fine, but you must follow it with R18 really - I'll include a variation for you to play with
C[topazg: Debated whether to connect here. I decided the corner was big enough (and weak enough if I didn't play here) to justify connecting
topazg: letting go of sente at this stage in the game is always a bit painful
C[topazg: Points I'm thinking about for White at the moment are J16, H17, E3, R12, K4, E10
C[topazg: K4 is a nice idea to prevent White making lower territory. Now you need to think somewhat about group stability
topazg: The G4+G3 exchanges don't help you all that much unless you're seriously attacking

C[topazg: As a rule you probably don't want to tenuki - I know G15 is a pretty local and attacking looking move, but a contact play against a single stone nearly always requires a direct response, generally a hane one side or the other
topazg: Especially when it's an invasion of your area, as you cannot afford to make it too easy for White to live

C[topazg: So the first discussion is overconcentration - The balance between spreading stones out too far (covering lots of the board, but getting no territory), and spreading out too little (getting very secure and solid territory, but not enough of it)
C[Fabian_LatAm2: Yes, I often myself in the doubt between strengthening weak stones or trying to gain influence in a larger portion of the board

C[topazg: Yes, it is a hard balance. Playing stones on the 5th line (as E6 is) can be quite risky. The aji (bad potential left for your opponent to exploit) is quite bad, you need to think specifically about how to deal with me playing behind it at C7, C5 or particularly C6.
C[topazg: I continue to try to occupy as many of the board edges as possible
C[Fabian_LatAm2: True. I Tend to take more risks in 13x13 but not very good ones apparently
Fabian_LatAm2: Now it seems I have only potential in the left side and that in the rest of the board you have a lot of influence
C[topazg: Yes, I have the potential to get a lot more points now just because I'm on  three of the four sides (we have two corners each)

C[Fabian_LatAm2: I am thinking about F12, E11 or G4: do you think any of these moves is good?

C[topazg: I like all three. The danger with F12 is that I can play E12 and cut it off, but I don't see any way of doing this. If I can't cut it off, F12 is fine. E11 is also ok, but after I blcok at F11, I suspect it's a slightly worse version of F12. G4 is great if you're ahead, and not so good if you're behind, as it will a) prevent me making more territory, and b) secure the territory I already have - this is fine if I have less territory than you, but bad if I have more, as it isn't really making you any points. Other ideas worth exploring tactically are K6 and L8. I will let you do some reading on those :)

column1: topazg: No. Honestly I almost wouldn't bother. By 4-6d, I'm sure AI analysis is exceptionally useful. At my level it's very handy, particularly for how to play local tactical situations. But ""why does the AI tenuki here and play over there, especially now"" is well above my understanding... and if you can't understand it, there's not much value in the analysis

topazg: I would have said you want to be at least 5k-1d before you really start getting a lot of value from the the AI - by all means do the site supporter thing, it's still free AI game reviews and gives you material to look at, but as you say, the interpretation is hard

topazg: Real players who can explain things in terms of principles and context from the perspective of another go player is generally more useful until your own understanding is basically at dan level

Sunny Goodman: I thought a pincer would cause a 3.3 in the top left, so thought this one.

C[topazg: Yes. It probably would mean I play the 3-3. The kick makes me slightly stronger on the left, and slightly protects the 3-3. I can actually still invade the 3-3 at this point, although at some cost on the left
topazg: I would generally rather player F17 directly than kick first unless I plan on playing B14 imminently, but as long as you aware you are gaining and losing something from the kick, it's fine
C[Sunny Goodman: I see, so the invasion can still happen. But yes, I thought of it as an exchange.
C[topazg: Yes, indeed :) Otherwise your opening is absolutely fine, nothing to say really
Sunny Goodman: I recently learned these opennings, so getting familiar with when to use each one. Bit of strategy.
C[topazg: Yes, this is a pretty important basic joseki. It used to be more common before AI made the low approach so much more often, but this is obviously still excellent, and simple to understand

C[topazg: Torn slightly which side to approach on - I chose this side as I'm happy with the slide+D8 joseki, as D8 offers a more solid left hand side than the equivalent shape would on the lower side. I'm equally happy with the 3-3, and if you kick I get the ideal extension from C10 on both sides

C[topazg: Because you have no stone on the lower side to support the high move at F4, I'm going to take this point immediately. It destabilises the corner somewhat and forces you to fight or make some concessions (or both)

C[topazg: I have chosen to respond to your defence with one of my own. I also thought about D8 as it takes away some of the aji I have left at C8 and D7, but I also can now aim towards C3 _and_ E3 myself, and I felt this was slightly more important
C[Sunny Goodman: I was wondering how it would happen.

C[topazg: F3 probably protects all of the aji - If I play E3, you can block with E4 and my stone just dies. It also means that if I play C3, you can block at C4 and the F3 stone stops me having enough space in the corner to make life

topazg: The most critical point of the last few moves is how you've felt compelled to defend ""internally"". The more I can force you to respond to defend whilst taking moves on the outside that I want to play that develop my position, the more I should be able to exploit the game's opening

topazg: The other options you have would be to play C8/D7/K3 etc instead of holding onto the corner, and try to apply as much pressure to me as I'm doing to you. The chances are that I play C3 and try to take the corner, but then you should get a strong outside position. You risk being left with a small enough number of points this way that you would probably get more profit from outside thickness in the end
C[Sunny Goodman: Ok, definitely felt obliged to do something, just wanted maybe live small and be done with it quickly. Haven't learned to invade yet, but started to see where the positions are.

topazg: Q17 is fine. Q18 also fine. As K16 is where it is, you could also leave the corner for a bit - if I jump into say, M17, you can just sacrifice O17 and play R17 quite happily too. This is fine as well, it is just a different commitment
C[Sunny Goodman: I think Q18 would be better as I don't want to follow this joseki as white will use it to make influence.
Sunny Goodman: I made that mistake yesterday, still getting familiar with these.
topazg: Q18 is certainly very common. I think Q17 is fine, but you must follow it with R18 really - I'll include a variation for you to play with
C[topazg: Debated whether to connect here. I decided the corner was big enough (and weak enough if I didn't play here) to justify connecting
topazg: letting go of sente at this stage in the game is always a bit painful
C[topazg: Points I'm thinking about for White at the moment are J16, H17, E3, R12, K4, E10
C[topazg: K4 is a nice idea to prevent White making lower territory. Now you need to think somewhat about group stability
topazg: The G4+G3 exchanges don't help you all that much unless you're seriously attacking

C[topazg: As a rule you probably don't want to tenuki - I know G15 is a pretty local and attacking looking move, but a contact play against a single stone nearly always requires a direct response, generally a hane one side or the other
topazg: Especially when it's an invasion of your area, as you cannot afford to make it too easy for White to live

C[topazg: Getting a good result in this corner is probably the most important. With a 3-4 (D17) and approach (C15), it is very large to play the next move locally. I would prefer a pincer like C13 or E16 to the C16 kick, but you could also play Q5/R5 or one of the many other approaches elsewhere too
topazg: As a rule, going on the attack is just as good at defending if you gain as many points (or take away as many of your opponents points) when you do so. It depends on the exact sequences
topazg: The other problem with the kick, is you also leave weakness (Black playing E17 next is uncomfortable for White)
topazg: So playing Q5 is fine if you end up in sente, but it might be painful if you end in gote and I get to play in the top left corner before you do

topazg: I think you're probably between c) and d). The game feels fairly close now. You've definitely lost most, if not all of the three stone handicap. My upper left is a decent number of points, but the lower left and lower right corners aren't all that huge. topazg: How good a move is locally in the midgame is hugely affected by how it affects the board globally. For example :

topazg: Move B1 seems to be locally very poor - why play it just to sacrifice it, and give White clear points up there? But globally, you're exploiting White's desire to make the group definitely alive to build a very large potential for points in the centre of the board

topazg: Naturally, I would look for stronger ways to resist than just trading one black stone for giving you that strength, but the point is that locally Black plays a move that he doesn't even intend to keep, because globally its sacrifice has so much more value
